section.title: Solomon Secures the Throne
section.sectionRationale: With Adonijah's coup put down in chapter 1, David gives his final charge to Solomon before he dies, calling him to covenant faithfulness and to settle unfinished business, showing loyal love to Barzillai's sons and answering for the old bloodguilt of Joab and the curses of Shimei. After David's death, Adonijah's request for Abishag reopens the question of the throne, and Solomon moves decisively: Adonijah is executed, Abiathar is exiled from the priesthood, Joab is struck down at the altar, and Shimei is finally put to death when he breaks his sworn word. The chapter closes with the kingdom firmly established in Solomon's hand.
section.thesis: David's dying charge to walk faithfully before the LORD and to deal rightly with unresolved bloodguilt and loyal love becomes the standard by which Solomon governs, so that every remaining threat to the throne, Adonijah's renewed ambition, Joab's old crimes, and Shimei's broken oath, is answered with real justice, and the kingdom is firmly established under the LORD's blessing rather than by Solomon's raw power.
section.frameNote: Five beats: David's charge to Solomon, verses 1 to 9; David's reign ends and Solomon's kingdom begins, verses 10 to 12; the execution of Adonijah, verses 13 to 25; the execution of Abiathar and Joab, verses 26 to 35; and the execution of Shimei with the kingdom secured, verses 36 to 46.

verses.text: As the time drew near for David to die, he charged his son Solomon,
“I am about to go the way of all the earth. So be strong and prove yourself a man.

And keep the charge of the LORD your God to walk in His ways and to keep His statutes, commandments, ordinances, and decrees, as written in the Law of Moses, so that you may prosper in all you do and wherever you turn,

and so that the LORD may fulfill His promise to me: ‘If your descendants take heed to walk faithfully before Me with all their heart and soul, you will never fail to have a man on the {{kisse|throne}} of Israel.’

Moreover, you know what Joab son of Zeruiah did to me, what he did to Abner son of Ner and Amasa son of Jether, the two commanders of the armies of Israel. He killed them in peacetime to avenge the blood of war. He stained with the blood of war the belt around his waist and the sandals on his feet.
So act according to your wisdom, and do not let his gray head go down to Sheol in peace.
But show {{chesed|loving devotion}} to the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite, and let them be among those who eat at your table, because they stood by me when I fled from your brother Absalom.

Keep an eye on Shimei the son of Gera, the Benjamite from Bahurim who is with you. He called down bitter curses against me on the day I went to Mahanaim, but when he came down to meet me at the Jordan, I swore to him by the LORD: ‘I will never put you to the sword.’
Now therefore, do not hold him guiltless, for you are a wise man. You know what you ought to do to him to bring his gray head down to Sheol in blood.”

units.label: David's reign ends, Solomon's kingdom begins

verses.text: Then David rested with his fathers and was buried in the City of David.
The length of David's reign over Israel was forty years, seven years in Hebron and thirty-three years in Jerusalem.
So Solomon sat on the {{kisse|throne}} of his father David, and his kingdom was firmly established.

units.label: The execution of Adonijah
units.note: a request that costs him his life

verses.text: Now Adonijah son of Haggith went to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon, and she asked, “Do you come in peace?” “Yes, in peace,” he replied.
Then he said, “I have something to tell you.” “Say it,” she answered.
“You know that the kingship was mine,” he said. “All Israel expected that I should reign, but the kingship has turned to my brother, for it has come to him from the LORD.
So now I have just one request of you; do not deny me.” “State your request,” she told him.
Adonijah replied, “Please speak to King Solomon, since he will not turn you down. Let him give me Abishag the Shunammite as my wife.”
“Very well,” Bathsheba replied. “I will speak to the king for you.”

So Bathsheba went to King Solomon to speak to him for Adonijah. The king stood up to greet her, bowed to her, and sat down on his throne. Then the king had a throne brought for his mother, who sat down at his right hand.
“I have just one small request of you,” she said. “Do not deny me.” “Make your request, my mother,” the king replied, “for I will not deny you.”
So Bathsheba said, “Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to your brother Adonijah as his wife.”

King Solomon answered his mother, “Why do you request Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? Since he is my older brother, you might as well request the kingdom for him and for Abiathar the priest and for Joab son of Zeruiah!”
Then King Solomon swore by the LORD: “May God punish me, and ever so severely, if Adonijah has not made this request at the expense of his life.

And now, as surely as the LORD lives, the One who established me, who set me on the {{kisse|throne}} of my father David, and who founded for me a dynasty as He promised, surely Adonijah shall be put to death today!”
So King Solomon gave orders to Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and he struck down Adonijah and he died.

units.label: The execution of Abiathar and Joab

verses.text: Then the king said to Abiathar the priest, “Go back to your fields in Anathoth. Even though you deserve to die, I will not put you to death at this time, since you carried the ark of the Lord GOD before my father David, and you suffered through all that my father suffered.”
So Solomon banished Abiathar from the priesthood of the LORD and thus fulfilled the word that the LORD had spoken at Shiloh against the house of Eli.
When the news reached Joab, who had conspired with Adonijah but not with Absalom, he fled to the tent of the LORD and took hold of the horns of the altar.
It was reported to King Solomon: “Joab has fled to the tent of the LORD and is now beside the altar.” So Solomon sent Benaiah son of Jehoiada, saying, “Go, strike him down!”

And Benaiah entered the tent of the LORD and said to Joab, “The king says, ‘Come out!’” But Joab replied, “No, I will die here.” So Benaiah relayed the message to the king, saying, “This is how Joab answered me.”
And the king replied, “Do just as he says. Strike him down and bury him, and so remove from me and from the house of my father the innocent blood that Joab shed.

The LORD will bring his bloodshed back upon his own head, for without the knowledge of my father David he struck down two men more righteous and better than he when he put to the sword Abner son of Ner, commander of Israel’s army, and Amasa son of Jether, commander of Judah’s army.

Their {{dam|blood}} will come back upon the heads of Joab and his descendants forever; but for David, his descendants, his house, and his throne, there shall be {{shalom|peace}} from the LORD forever.”
So Benaiah son of Jehoiada went up, struck down Joab, and killed him. He was buried at his own home in the wilderness.
And the king appointed Benaiah son of Jehoiada in Joab’s place over the army, and he appointed Zadok the priest in Abiathar’s place.

units.label: The execution of Shimei, the kingdom secured
units.note: a broken oath, a firm throne

verses.text: Then the king summoned Shimei and said to him, “Build a house for yourself in Jerusalem and live there, but do not go anywhere else.
On the day you go out and cross the Kidron Valley, know for sure that you will die; your blood will be on your own head.”
“The sentence is fair,” Shimei replied. “Your servant will do as my lord the king has spoken.” And Shimei lived in Jerusalem for a long time.
After three years, however, two of Shimei’s slaves ran away to Achish son of Maacah, king of Gath. And Shimei was told, “Look, your slaves are in Gath.”
So Shimei saddled his donkey and set out to Achish at Gath in search of his slaves, and he brought them back from Gath.
When Solomon was told that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath and had returned,

the king summoned Shimei and said to him, “Did I not make you swear by the LORD and warn you, ‘On the day you leave and go elsewhere, know for sure that you will die’? And you told me, ‘The sentence is fair; I will comply.’
So why have you not kept your oath to the LORD and the command that I gave you?”
The king also said, “You know in your heart all the evil that you did to my father David. Therefore the LORD will bring your evil back upon your head.
But King Solomon will be blessed and David’s throne will remain secure before the LORD forever.”
Then the king commanded Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and he went out and struck Shimei down, and he died. Thus the kingdom was firmly established in the hand of Solomon.

kisse.gloss: Throne, the seat of Davidic royal authority. David's charge to Solomon turns repeatedly on this word: the LORD's promise of a man forever on Israel's kisse, David's own claim to have been set on it by the LORD, and the final declaration that David's throne will remain secure forever. What Adonijah tried to seize by presumption in chapter 1, Solomon now secures under God's own hand, a shadow of the everlasting throne promised to great David's greater Son, Luke 1:32-33.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, covenant loyalty, kindness that keeps faith even when it is not strictly owed. David instructs Solomon to show chesed to the sons of Barzillai, who risked themselves for David in exile; loyal love, not merely bare justice, is to mark this new reign, foreshadowing the far greater loving devotion God shows His own covenant people.

dam.gloss: Blood, especially blood shed unjustly, or bloodguilt crying out for reckoning. The chapter is saturated with dam: Joab's shedding of innocent blood in peacetime, and Solomon's declaration that it will come back upon Joab's own head rather than upon David's house. This bloodguilt that must be answered anticipates the deeper reckoning for sin finally satisfied only by the blood of Christ, Hebrews 9:22.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being that flows from right relationship with God. Even amid these executions of justice, the chapter closes by declaring that David's house, descendants, and throne will have shalom from the LORD forever, a peace secured not by ignoring wrongdoing but by dealing with it rightly, pointing forward to the true and lasting peace established through Christ, Isaiah 9:6-7.

basic.context: As David nears death, he charges Solomon to walk faithfully before the LORD and to settle unfinished business: kindness for Barzillai's sons, and justice for the old bloodguilt of Joab and the curses of Shimei. After David dies and Solomon's kingdom is established, Adonijah requests Abishag as his wife, a request Solomon reads as a renewed bid for the throne, and has him executed. Abiathar is exiled from the priesthood, Joab is struck down at the altar, and Shimei is finally put to death years later when he breaks his sworn word. The chapter closes with the kingdom firmly established in Solomon's hand. The key questions are why David's charge singles out these particular men, whether Solomon's actions are just or vengeful, and how the chapter's violence fits within God's covenant faithfulness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing the settling of specific old scores under ancient Near Eastern kingship, not a manual for handling personal grievances today. What endures as the pattern for God's people is that bloodguilt and covenant loyalty both matter to the LORD, that mercy and justice are not opposites but companions rightly apportioned, and that God's kingdom purposes advance even through the messy business of establishing a fragile throne.
basic.originalLanguages: David's charge to Solomon turns on the kisse, the throne, promised to an obedient dynasty forever. He asks Solomon to show chesed, loving devotion, to Barzillai's sons, while unresolved dam, blood, from Joab's old crimes must finally be reckoned with. The chapter closes by declaring that despite the bloodshed required to secure it, David's house and throne will know shalom, peace, from the LORD forever.
basic.typology: David's charge that Solomon's throne be established through the removal of bloodguilt and the reward of loyal love points toward Christ's throne, established once for all through His own perfect righteousness and covenant faithfulness. Solomon's mixture of mercy toward Abiathar and justice toward Joab and Shimei anticipates the true King who is both merciful to those who trust Him and just toward all unrepentant rebellion.
basic.christ: Solomon secures his throne only once every rival claim and unresolved bloodguilt has been dealt with rightly, sparing Abiathar in mercy while executing justice on Joab and Shimei. Christ's kingdom is established forever once He has dealt fully and finally with sin itself, both showing mercy to all who come to Him in faith and executing perfect justice on unrepentant evil, 1 Corinthians 15:24-25, Romans 3:25-26.
basic.god: God is faithful to the covenant He swore to David, working even through the difficult business of settling old bloodguilt and testing loyalty. He values loving devotion shown to the faithful, He does not overlook innocent blood shed unjustly, and He secures peace for His people not by ignoring wrongdoing but by seeing it rightly answered.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 35:33 explains why unavenged bloodshed pollutes the land and demands reckoning. 2 Samuel 16:20-22 supplies the background for why claiming a king's concubine was read as a bid for the throne. 1 Samuel 2:31-35 records the earlier prophecy against the house of Eli that Abiathar's banishment now fulfills. And Luke 1:32-33 points to the throne of David finally established forever in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The Davidic covenant promised an enduring throne, but that throne first had to survive its own founding generation's unfinished business, old bloodguilt, broken oaths, and renewed ambition. Solomon's decisive justice and selective mercy secure the kingdom for now, and the chapter's repeated language of an eternal, peaceful throne quietly points beyond Solomon to Jesus, the Son of David whose kingdom will never need to be secured again because it can never be threatened.

context.literary: The chapter moves from private instruction, David's charge in his own chamber, to a rapid sequence of public reckonings: Adonijah, Abiathar, Joab, and Shimei each receive judgment in turn, building toward the closing summary that the kingdom was firmly established. The repeated language of bloodguilt going down to Sheol, echoed across the charge and its fulfillment, gives the chapter a tight, deliberate structure of promise and execution.
context.historical: Ancient Near Eastern successions were commonly unstable, and a new king's early reign was often marked by removing rivals and settling old debts precisely as Solomon does here. Taking a former king's concubine was widely understood as a claim to his throne, as Absalom had shown publicly with David's own concubines, which is why Solomon reads Adonijah's request for Abishag as treasonous. Grasping the horns of the altar, as Joab does, was a recognized plea for sanctuary, though it did not guarantee immunity from a king determined to execute justice.
context.canonical: This chapter closes the founding generation of the united monarchy and clears the way for Solomon's reign proper, described from chapter 3 onward. It also fulfills earlier prophetic words, the judgment on Eli's house spoken in 1 Samuel 2, and it reinforces the conditional element within the unconditional Davidic promise, obedience secures blessing, while the throne itself remains secured by God's covenant faithfulness.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Solomon's throne is established not merely by strength but by faithfully carrying out David's charge, honoring loyal love where it is due and answering bloodguilt and broken oaths where justice requires it, so that the kingdom is firmly secured under the LORD's blessing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes specific ancient acts of justice carried out by a particular king in a particular political crisis; it does not prescribe capital punishment as a template for every grievance today. What endures is the principle that bloodguilt matters to God, that loyalty deserves loyalty, and that a throne established on genuine justice, not mere power, is the throne God blesses.

debates.question: Was David's charge to Solomon regarding Joab and Shimei righteous justice or personal vengeance?

views.label: Righteous justice finally administered
views.case: David frames the charge in terms of covenant faithfulness, verses 2 to 4, and unresolved bloodguilt he as king had been unable to address, echoing his own earlier lament that 'these sons of Zeruiah are too violent for me,' 2 Samuel 3:39; Numbers 35:33 required that innocent blood be answered, and David charges Solomon, now unencumbered, to finally administer the justice due.

views.label: A concern of personal vendetta
views.case: Critics note the timing, delivered on David's deathbed, and the personal language, 'what he did to me,' which could suggest old scores being settled rather than disinterested justice, especially since David himself had sworn not to harm Shimei, verse 8, and now effectively passes that unfulfilled reckoning on to his son.

debates.bearing: Either way, the narrative presents Solomon's later actions as measured and deliberate, distinguishing mercy for Abiathar from justice for Joab and Shimei, and frames the outcome as securing the LORD's promised peace over David's house rather than arbitrary bloodshed.

debates.question: Why did Solomon interpret Adonijah's request for Abishag as treasonous?

views.label: A recognized ancient claim to kingship
views.case: In the ancient Near East, taking a former king's wife or concubine symbolically claimed his throne, exactly as Absalom had done publicly with David's concubines, 2 Samuel 16:21-22; so Solomon rightly read the outwardly modest request as a renewed bid for kingship, especially so soon after Adonijah's earlier coup attempt, verse 22.

views.label: An overreaction to a comparatively innocent request
views.case: Some read Solomon's response as harsh, given Adonijah had already submitted and been pardoned in chapter 1; on this view, Solomon's swift justice reflects the ruthless self-preservation common to kings securing a fragile new throne, more than a genuinely treasonous act by Adonijah.

debates.bearing: The text itself sides with Solomon's explanation, verse 22, tying the request directly to the kingdom, and situates Solomon's justice within the larger pattern of David's charge being fulfilled rather than abused.

study.originalLanguages: David's charge returns again and again to the kisse, the throne, promised to a faithful dynasty forever. He calls Solomon to show chesed, loving devotion, to Barzillai's sons who once risked themselves for him, while unresolved dam, blood, from Joab's old murders in peacetime must at last be reckoned with rather than left to stain David's house. The chapter's closing words promise that, once justice is rightly done, David's throne will know shalom, peace, from the LORD forever, showing that true peace and unaddressed injustice cannot coexist.

typology.type: David's charge that Solomon's throne be established through removing bloodguilt and rewarding loyal love
typology.fulfillment: Christ's throne established once for all through His own righteous judgment of sin and perfect covenant faithfulness.

typology.type: Solomon showing mercy to Abiathar, exile rather than death, while executing justice on Joab and Shimei
typology.fulfillment: The true King's perfect balance of mercy and justice, fulfilled in Christ, who justifies the guilty who trust Him and will judge all unrepentant rebellion, Romans 3:25-26.

typology.type: The kingdom firmly established in Solomon's hand once every rival and unresolved guilt is dealt with
typology.fulfillment: The kingdom of Christ, established forever once He has dealt fully and finally with sin, 1 Corinthians 15:24-25.

study.christ: Solomon's throne is not secure merely because he holds power, but because rightful justice and rightful mercy are both carried out, mercy for Abiathar's long faithful service, justice for Joab's old innocent blood and Shimei's broken oath. Christ's throne is established in exactly this pattern taken to its perfection: He shows boundless mercy to all who come to Him in humble faith, and He will execute perfect and final justice on all who persist in rebellion, Acts 17:31, Romans 3:25-26. Where Solomon's peace depended on justice finally being done within his own house, the peace of Christ's kingdom rests on justice fully satisfied at the cross, Colossians 1:19-20.
study.god: God is faithful to His covenant with David even through the difficult, bloody business of establishing a throne among sinful people. He does not treat innocent blood as a small thing; it must be reckoned with rather than left to pollute a king's house. He values loyal love shown to the faithful, as with Barzillai's sons, and He secures true peace for His people not by overlooking wrongdoing but by seeing that it is rightly answered, so that David's line can stand secure before Him forever.

crossReferences.note: Innocent bloodshed pollutes the land and must be atoned for, the law behind David's insistence that Joab's crimes be answered.
David's earlier restraint toward Joab, calling the sons of Zeruiah 'too violent for me,' background to the unresolved bloodguilt now addressed.
Absalom's public claim on David's concubines, the background custom explaining why Adonijah's request for Abishag reads as treason.
The LORD's earlier judgment spoken against the house of Eli, fulfilled when Solomon banishes Abiathar from the priesthood.
The angel's promise that Jesus will be given the throne of His father David and reign forever, the ultimate horizon of this chapter's securing of the throne.

study.oneStory: The Davidic covenant promised an enduring throne, but first that throne had to survive the unfinished business of its founding generation, old bloodguilt, broken oaths, and a rival's renewed ambition. Solomon's decisive justice and selective mercy secure the kingdom in his own day, while the chapter's closing promise of a throne established with peace forever quietly points beyond Solomon to Jesus, the greater Son of David, whose kingdom needs no further securing because sin itself has been dealt with fully and finally at the cross.

sermon.title: Death of the King, Part Two
sermon.why: Preached from 1 Kings 2:5-12, this message examines David's charge to Solomon regarding Joab and Shimei, considering whether it was personal vengeance or the responsibility of God's anointed king to administer justice, and points forward to Christ's own final and fair judgment as King.

quiz.q: What is the central charge David gives Solomon as he nears death, verses 1-4?

options.text: To be strong, to keep the LORD's charge by walking in His ways and statutes, so that the LORD's promise of an enduring dynasty would be fulfilled.
options.note: This is the opening and central content of David's charge, verses 2-4.

options.text: To avenge every wrong done to David personally before doing anything else.
options.note: This is part of the charge regarding Joab and Shimei specifically, verses 5-9, but it is not the central, opening charge, which is covenant faithfulness.

options.text: To immediately go to war against neighboring nations to expand the kingdom.
options.note: Not found anywhere in the text of David's charge.

options.text: To ignore the Law of Moses and rule purely by his own wisdom.
options.note: The opposite of verse 3, which charges Solomon to keep the Law of Moses.

quiz.q: Why does David instruct Solomon not to let Joab die peacefully, verses 5-6, 31-33?

options.text: Because Joab shed innocent blood in peacetime, killing Abner and Amasa, and that bloodguilt needed to be answered rather than passed on to David's house forever.
options.note: Verses 31-33 give this exact reasoning for Joab's execution.

options.text: Because Joab had personally insulted David in public.
options.note: Not the stated reason; the issue is bloodguilt from murder, not personal insult.

options.text: Because Joab supported Adonijah's attempted coup, and this is the primary reason for his death.
options.note: Joab's support of Adonijah, verse 28, affects the timing of his death, but David's original charge, given before any coup, is rooted in the earlier bloodguilt of Abner and Amasa.

options.text: Because Joab had failed to win a military campaign for David.
options.note: Not found in the text; Joab's offense is murder, not military failure.

quiz.q: What does Solomon do differently with Abiathar compared to Joab and Shimei, and why?

options.text: Solomon spares Abiathar's life and only exiles him from the priesthood, because of his long, faithful service carrying the ark with David, even though his support of Adonijah deserved death.
options.note: Verses 26-27 explicitly distinguish Abiathar's mercy from the fate of Joab and Shimei.

options.text: Solomon executes Abiathar along with Joab because both had supported Adonijah equally.
options.note: False; the text explicitly spares Abiathar's life, exiling rather than executing him, verse 26.

options.text: Solomon restores Abiathar to an even higher priestly office as a reward.
options.note: False; he is banished from the priesthood, not promoted, verse 27.

options.text: Abiathar is never mentioned again after supporting Adonijah's feast.
options.note: False; his fate and its fulfillment of prophecy against Eli's house are explicitly recorded, verses 26-27.

quiz.q: Why is Shimei ultimately put to death, years after Solomon's initial restriction on him, verses 36-46?

options.text: Shimei broke his sworn oath by leaving Jerusalem to retrieve his runaway slaves from Gath, violating the LORD-sworn condition Solomon had set.
options.note: Verses 40-46 record exactly this sequence of events and Solomon's stated reasoning.

options.text: Shimei cursed Solomon publicly a second time.
options.note: Not in the text; his offense here is leaving Jerusalem, not renewed cursing of the king.

options.text: Shimei tried to help Adonijah escape from Solomon's justice.
options.note: Not stated as the reason for Shimei's death in this passage.

options.text: Shimei refused to build a house in Jerusalem as commanded.
options.note: False; he did build a house and lived there for a long time, verse 38.

quiz.q: How does the chapter summarize the result of Solomon's actions, verse 46?

options.text: The kingdom was firmly established in the hand of Solomon.
options.note: This is the exact closing statement of the chapter, verse 46.

options.text: Solomon became wealthy beyond all other kings as a direct result of these executions.
options.note: That theme of wealth belongs to chapter 3, not to this chapter's own closing summary.

options.text: The people rebelled once more shortly afterward.
options.note: Contrary to the text's own summary of a firmly established kingdom.

options.text: David's line effectively ended with Solomon since he had no heir mentioned.
options.note: False; the entire chapter is about securing, not ending, the Davidic dynastic promise.

questions: David's charge to Solomon centers on walking faithfully before the LORD, not merely on political survival. Where in your own life is faithfulness to God being crowded out by concerns for security or self-preservation?
David asked Solomon to remember loyal love toward those who stood by him in hard times, Barzillai's sons. Who has shown you costly loyalty that you have not yet properly honored?
Joab's old, unresolved bloodguilt finally had to be answered. Is there an unresolved wrong in your own life or relationships that you have been avoiding rather than addressing honestly?

Solomon showed real mercy to Abiathar because of his past faithfulness, even though he deserved worse. Where might God be calling you to extend mercy to someone whose past service outweighs their present failure?
Shimei's death came only after he broke a sworn oath he had willingly accepted. What promises or commitments have you made to God that you are tempted to quietly break when it becomes inconvenient?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote observes that Jether is a variant of Ithra, the name given for Amasa's father in 2 Samuel 17:25; the same variant recurs in verse 32.

The BSB footnote explains that forms of the Hebrew chesed are rendered 'loving devotion' throughout Scripture, a word whose range includes love, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and loyalty to a covenant.
The BSB footnote notes that Maacah is a variant of Maoch, the name given for the king of Gath's father in 1 Samuel 27:2.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 2:1-46. Three em dashes in the source text, verses 5, 11, and 24, have been converted to commas so that no em dashes or en dashes remain anywhere in this file.
